meta.title: D2 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Land Rover Discovery 2 engine — Td5, 4.0 V8, 4.6 V8. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and a clear 60% scrap threshold. Get free quotes from 12 vetted Land Rover specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Land Rover Discovery 2 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned Discovery 2 engine typically costs £1,250–£5,500 supply only [EM-QUOTE], depending on engine — the Td5 diesel sits at £1,250–£2,800, while the V8s range from £2,500 to £5,500. Fitting adds £650–£1,000. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which Land Rover Discovery 2 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The Td5 five-cylinder diesel (10P/15P/16P) is the most dependable — it offers strong torque, simpler mechanicals than the V8s, and avoids the porous bore liner issues that plague the Rover V8 petrol units. Its main weakness is the injector wiring harness, which is a well‑understood, affordable fix.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Land Rover Discovery 2?

acceptedAnswer.text: Common issues include: Td5 injector harness oil contamination leading to ECU faults; V8 porous cylinder liners causing coolant loss and misfires; and rear chassis corrosion — the most frequent non‑engine failure. All are well documented across UK specialist forums.

mainEntity.name: Is the Land Rover Discovery 2 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on the engine and how it was maintained. The Td5 is generally reliable when its injector harness is kept in check; the V8s have a known block‑liner weakness that can be costly. Beyond the engine, the chassis is the biggest long‑term worry — check rust thoroughly before buying.

mainEntity.name: Which Discovery 2 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: We don’t recommend outright avoidance, but a V8 (4.0 or 4.6) without documented cooling system history is a risk — porous liners can lead to a full engine replacement. If you must have a V8, budget for a professional liner inspection and a cooling system overhaul.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my D2 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Key warning signs: Td5 — rough running, misfire, or oil in the ECU connector; V8 — persistent coolant loss, white smoke on start‑up, or misfire when cold. Use our diagnostic guide to identify the specific symptoms, and always get a specialist compression and coolant‑gas test.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Discovery 2 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Apply our 60% Land Rover threshold. A £2,800 Td5 recon against a £4,000 D2 is 70% — borderline, often not worth it. Against a well‑kept £6,000 example, the same recon is 47% — viable. For a V8, a £4,500 recon on a £4,000 car is a write‑off. Value the car first; if it exceeds 60%, walk away.

mainEntity.name: How long do Discovery 2 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: Td5 units can exceed 200,000 miles with regular servicing and harness attention. V8s can reach similar mileage, but liner issues often appear before 120,000 miles — proactive coolant changes and leak detection are critical. Chassis corrosion typically ends the vehicle’s life before the engine wears out.

mainEntity.name: Is the Discovery 2 a single body style?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — the Discovery 2 (L318) was produced exclusively as a 5‑door SUV. There are no saloon, coupé, or convertible siblings in this generation. The Discovery 3 (L319) is a separate generation with its own engine lineup, covered on its own page.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Discovery 2 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is stamped on the cylinder block — for Td5, look on the left‑hand side of the block (near the starter motor); for the Rover V8, it’s on the block face below the inlet manifold. Alternatively, check your VIN plate or vehicle documentation. Tell us your registration and we’ll confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Land Rover Discovery 2 D2 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the Discovery 2 — from the five-cylinder Td5 turbo-diesel to the 4.0 and 4.6-litre Rover V8s. Real UK replacement costs, honest failure data, and the coolant and liner issues that define this generation's ownership story. Backed by real UK enquiry data [PATTERN DATA].

engineDatabase.h2: Land Rover Discovery 2 D2 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the Discovery 2 specifically — including the mid-cycle 4.6-litre V8 upgrade that arrived for the 2003 model year.

confidenceScore.title: Confidence Score
confidenceScore.text: All three ratings are provisional — no dedicated Engine Page exists for any Discovery 2 engine family. The Td5 rating is derived from Honest John and Autocar buyer's guide data; the Rover V8 ratings reflect the well-documented porous bore liner and coolant leak issues. Confirm against Engine Pages once built.

overview.intro: The Discovery 2 — often referred to as the Series II — was launched in 1998 as the successor to the original Discovery. Though it retained the 100-inch wheelbase and live-axle chassis of its predecessor, Land Rover claimed 85% of the vehicle was new. It introduced the all-new Td5 five-cylinder diesel engine — a significant step up from the 300 Tdi — alongside the familiar Rover V8 petrol. A 2003 facelift brought a 4.6-litre V8 upgrade, restyled lights, and the return of the centre-diff lock as an option. Production ended in May 2004.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Discovery 2 / L318 (live axle, 100-inch wheelbase) • Predecessor: Discovery 1 • Successor: Discovery 3 / LR3 • Sibling body styles in this generation: none — D2 is a single body style.
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The Discovery 2 remains a popular choice among UK off-road enthusiasts, with Td5 diesel variants commanding the strongest demand [PATTERN DATA].

items.quote: Thin-Data Fallback mode — exactly one paragraph: The Td5 five-cylinder diesel is the most dependable Discovery 2 engine — a 136 bhp unit that offers strong low-end torque and, unlike the Rover V8, avoids the porous bore liner issues that plague the petrol units. The 4.0 and 4.6-litre V8s are the engines to approach with caution: they're smooth and characterful, but coolant leaks and porous cylinder liners are well-documented weaknesses that can lead to expensive repairs. For buyers, the Td5 is the sensible choice; the V8s are for enthusiasts with a healthy budget for maintenance.

ownershipEconomics.h2: Land Rover Discovery 2 D2 — Ownership Economics

rows.commonMajorFailure: Injector wiring harness oil contamination; ECU ripple alternator failure
rows.ownershipVerdict: Best Diesel — the D2's most dependable engine, but watch the wiring loom.
High Risk — smooth, but liner failures can be catastrophic.

rows.commonMajorFailure: Porous bore liners; slipped cylinder liners
rows.ownershipVerdict: High Risk — same issues as 4.0, more expensive to replace.

economicsRule.title: The D2 rule
economicsRule.text: A £2,800 Td5 recon against a £4,000 Discovery 2 sits at 70% of the car's value — brushing our 60% Land Rover threshold. On a sub-£3,000 D2, that recon is simply not viable. The V8s are even worse: a £4,500+ recon against a £4,000 car is a financial write-off. Value the vehicle first, then the repair.

keyTakeaways.question: Which D2 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: Td5 — cheaper to run, cheaper to fix, and no porous liner risk.

keyTakeaways.question: Which D2 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: 4.6 V8 — higher fuel consumption, higher replacement cost, same failure risks.

keyTakeaways.question: Which D2 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: Td5 with documented injector harness history — the sensible buy.

problems.title: Td5 Injector Wiring Harness Oil Contamination
problems.rootCause: Engine oil can wick up the injector wiring harness from the injector seals, contaminating the ECU connector and causing erratic running, misfires, and eventually ECU failure. This is the Td5's most common electrical fault.

tieredCosts.work: Replace injector harness and seals; clean ECU connector
Full injector harness and ECU replacement
tieredCosts.note: Expensive but still viable on a good Td5

problems.recommendation: At the first sign of rough running or misfire on a Td5, inspect the injector harness and ECU connector — a £300–£600 preventative fix is far cheaper than the £800–£1,500 ECU replacement that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare Td5 engine prices for your Discovery 2 →

problems.title: Rover V8 Porous Cylinder Liners
problems.rootCause: The Rover V8's cylinder liners can become porous or slip, allowing coolant to enter the combustion chamber. The result is persistent coolant loss, misfires on cold start, and eventually catastrophic engine failure. The 4.6-litre unit is particularly susceptible.
problems.recommendation: If your D2 V8 is consuming coolant with no external leak, have a specialist test for combustion gases in the coolant immediately. Ignoring this is the most expensive mistake you can make on a Discovery 2 — a £4,500+ replacement is rarely viable against a £3,000–£5,000 car.

cta.label: Compare Rover V8 engine prices for your Discovery 2 →

problems.title: Chassis Corrosion (Rear Section)
problems.affectedModels: All Discovery 2s, particularly high-mileage UK examples.
problems.rootCause: Unlike the Discovery 1, where the body rusted, the Discovery 2's chassis corrodes — particularly the rear section, which is vulnerable to off-road impacts and water ingress. Severe corrosion can write off an otherwise decent vehicle.
problems.recommendation: Have the rear chassis inspected at every MOT — surface rust is treatable; structural rot is a serious problem. A £300–£800 preventative repair is far cheaper than a £1,500+ chassis replacement.

replacementCosts.h2: D2 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance note required — no Engine Page baseline exists for these engines yet. Costs are sourced from UK specialist suppliers [EM-QUOTE].

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are Discovery 2 / D2 / L318 only. The Discovery 3 (L319) is a separate generation with different engines and is covered on its own Generation Page.
